title: Network Security Control Standards
category: Requirement 1: Network Security Controls
description: Processes and mechanisms for installing and maintaining network security controls are defined and understood.
purpose: Establishes the foundational policies for network security control implementation.
implementationGuidance: Define and document security policies and operational procedures for all network security controls. Assign roles and responsibilities. Review at least once every 12 months.
bestPractices: Review network security policies annually
Align with CIS Controls
Assign dedicated network security ownership

title: Network Security Control Configuration
category: Requirement 1: Network Security Controls
description: Network security controls (NSCs) are configured and maintained.
purpose: Ensures network security devices are configured securely to protect the cardholder data environment.
implementationGuidance: Implement and configure NSCs (firewalls, routers, cloud security groups) to protect the CDE. Define approved services, protocols, and ports. Review NSC rule sets at least every 6 months.
bestPractices: Block all traffic not explicitly needed
Review firewall rules every 6 months
Document the business justification for every rule

title: Network Access Restrictions to CDE
category: Requirement 1: Network Security Controls
description: Network access to and from the cardholder data environment is restricted.
purpose: Limits inbound and outbound network access to only what is necessary for business operations.
implementationGuidance: Configure NSCs to restrict all traffic to and from the CDE. Block all direct public access. Implement DMZ for public-facing components. Deny all other traffic by default.
evidenceExamples: Firewall rules showing CDE access restrictions
bestPractices: Apply principle of least privilege to all CDE network access
Use DMZ for all internet-facing components
Implement micro-segmentation within the CDE

title: Network Connections Between Trusted and Untrusted Networks
category: Requirement 1: Network Security Controls
description: Network connections between trusted and untrusted networks are controlled.
purpose: Protects the CDE from threats originating in untrusted network segments.
implementationGuidance: Deploy stateful inspection firewalls between trusted and untrusted networks. Implement anti-spoofing measures. Block private IP addresses from inbound internet traffic.
bestPractices: Use next-generation firewalls with application awareness
Implement egress filtering alongside ingress filtering
Monitor and alert on anomalous cross-boundary traffic

title: Risks from Computing Devices Connecting to Both Untrusted Networks and the CDE
category: Requirement 1: Network Security Controls
description: Risks to the CDE from computing devices that are able to connect to both untrusted networks and the CDE are mitigated.
purpose: Prevents devices that connect to untrusted networks from introducing threats into the CDE.
implementationGuidance: Implement split tunnelling controls or require all traffic through corporate VPN when accessing CDE. Deploy endpoint protection on all devices connecting to CDE. Use NAC to assess device security posture before CDE access.
bestPractices: Disable split tunnelling for devices accessing the CDE
Implement NAC to verify device health before CDE access
Enforce endpoint protection as a condition of CDE access

title: Processes for Managing All System Components
category: Requirement 2: Secure Configurations
description: Processes and mechanisms for applying secure configurations to all system components are defined and understood.
purpose: Ensures secure configurations are applied consistently to all system components in the CDE.
implementationGuidance: Define and document configuration standards for all system component types. Develop configuration baselines. Assign ownership. Review annually or when new components are added.
bestPractices: Base configuration standards on CIS Benchmarks
Automate configuration compliance checking
Review standards annually for security relevance

title: System Component Configurations
category: Requirement 2: Secure Configurations
description: System components are configured and managed securely.
purpose: Prevents configuration weaknesses from being exploited to access cardholder data.
implementationGuidance: Apply vendor-hardening guidelines and industry standards (CIS Benchmarks) to all system components. Remove or disable all unnecessary default accounts, passwords, functions, and services. Change all vendor-supplied default credentials before deployment.
evidenceExamples: No default credentials in production systems
bestPractices: Never deploy systems with default credentials
Automate hardening using configuration management tools (Ansible, Chef)
Run CIS Benchmark compliance scans after deployment

title: Wireless Environments
category: Requirement 2: Secure Configurations
description: Wireless environments are configured and managed securely.
purpose: Ensures wireless networks connected to the CDE or transmitting cardholder data are secured.
implementationGuidance: Change all wireless default SSID names, passwords, and SNMP community strings. Enable WPA3 or WPA2 with AES encryption. Implement wireless intrusion detection. Conduct quarterly wireless scans.
bestPractices: Separate wireless guest networks from CDE networks
Implement WPA3 where possible
Deploy a wireless intrusion detection system (WIDS)

title: Account Data Storage Processes
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting stored account data are defined and understood.
purpose: Ensures policies governing stored cardholder data are defined and maintained.
implementationGuidance: Define policies governing what account data can be stored, how it must be protected, and for how long. Document data flows and data storage locations. Review annually.
evidenceExamples: Data flow diagram showing stored CHD locations
bestPractices: Store only what is absolutely necessary
Automate deletion of account data at end of retention period
Conduct annual data storage reviews to identify unnecessary data

title: Storage of Sensitive Authentication Data
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored after authorisation.
purpose: Prevents storage of data that could enable fraudulent transactions.
implementationGuidance: Confirm that no SAD (full magnetic stripe data, CVV/CVV2, PINs) is stored after transaction authorisation. Implement technical controls to detect and prevent SAD storage. Verify with forensic methods.
evidenceExamples: Database scan results showing no SAD
Data discovery scan evidence for SAD
bestPractices: Never store full magnetic stripe data, CVV, or PIN data post-authorisation
Use tokenisation to eliminate SAD from the payment environment
Conduct regular data discovery scans to detect SAD storage

title: Sensitive Authentication Data Not Retained
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Sensitive authentication data is not stored after authorisation, even if encrypted.
purpose: Provides absolute prohibition on storing sensitive authentication data post-authorisation.
implementationGuidance: Implement technical controls that prevent any storage of SAD after authorisation completes. This applies even if the data would be encrypted. Audit code and configurations to verify compliance.
requiredDocuments: Code review evidence for SAD handling
evidenceExamples: Code review results for SAD handling
Authorisation flow diagrams showing SAD not retained
bestPractices: Review all code paths that touch payment data for SAD retention risks
Use point-to-point encryption (P2PE) to reduce SAD exposure
Train developers on SAD storage prohibitions

title: PAN Rendered Unreadable Anywhere It Is Stored
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Primary account numbers (PAN) are secured with strong cryptography wherever stored.
purpose: Ensures stored PANs cannot be read if storage media is compromised.
implementationGuidance: Implement one of: strong one-way hash functions of the entire PAN, truncation (first 6/last 4 digits), index tokens with secure pad, or strong cryptography using AES-256. Verify encryption is applied to all PAN storage locations.
requiredDocuments: PAN storage inventory with protection method
bestPractices: Use tokenisation to completely eliminate PAN from most systems
Apply AES-256 for any required PAN storage
Verify PAN protection for all storage locations including backups and logs

title: PAN Secured with Cryptography
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Primary account number is secured wherever it is stored with strong cryptography.
purpose: Provides protection for PANs using strong cryptographic algorithms and key management.
implementationGuidance: Implement AES-256 or equivalent strong cryptography for PAN protection. Implement comprehensive key management covering key generation, distribution, storage, rotation, and destruction.
bestPractices: Use a dedicated Key Management Service (KMS) or HSM for PAN encryption keys
Automate key rotation on defined schedules
Never store encryption keys in the same location as encrypted PAN

title: Cryptographic Keys Used to Protect Stored Account Data
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data are secured.
purpose: Ensures the encryption keys protecting PAN are themselves securely managed.
implementationGuidance: Protect encryption keys with key-encrypting keys (KEKs) stored separately. Limit key access to fewest custodians necessary. Prevent unauthorised substitution of keys. Implement dual control and split knowledge for key management.
requiredDocuments: Dual control and split knowledge procedures
bestPractices: Use HSMs for all key generation and storage
Implement split knowledge so no single person knows the complete key
Conduct annual key custodian reviews

title: Key Management Policies and Procedures
category: Requirement 3: Protect Stored Account Data
description: Where cryptography is used to protect stored account data, key management policies and procedures are in place.
purpose: Ensures comprehensive key lifecycle management is documented and implemented.
implementationGuidance: Define key management procedures covering all lifecycle stages: generation, distribution, storage, access, retirement, and destruction. Implement automated key rotation. Retire and replace keys on cryptoperiod expiry.
bestPractices: Define cryptoperiods for all key types following NIST SP 800-57
Automate key rotation to eliminate human error
Maintain key inventory documenting each key's purpose, algorithm, and retirement date

title: Processes for Protecting PAN with Strong Cryptography During Transmission
category: Requirement 4: Protect Cardholder Data with Strong Cryptography During Transmission
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting cardholder data with strong cryptography during transmission over open, public networks are defined and understood.
purpose: Ensures policies governing encryption of cardholder data in transit are documented.
implementationGuidance: Define policies requiring strong cryptography for all cardholder data transmitted over open, public networks. Document approved cryptographic standards and prohibited protocols. Review annually.
bestPractices: Approve only TLS 1.2 and 1.3; explicitly prohibit SSL, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1
Document all transmission paths carrying CHD
Review transmission encryption standards annually

title: PAN Protected with Strong Cryptography During Transmission
category: Requirement 4: Protect Cardholder Data with Strong Cryptography During Transmission
description: PAN is protected with strong cryptography during transmission over open, public networks.
purpose: Prevents cardholder data from being intercepted and read during transmission.
implementationGuidance: Implement TLS 1.2 or 1.3 for all transmission of PAN over open networks. Never send unprotected PAN via email, instant messaging, or chat. Obtain trusted certificates from a trusted CA.
requiredDocuments: Prohibited transmission channels for unprotected PAN
evidenceExamples: TLS 1.2/1.3 configuration evidence on all CHD transmission paths
bestPractices: Test TLS configurations using SSL Labs
Implement certificate pinning for mobile payment applications
Prohibit sending PAN via email in all internal policies

title: Processes for Protecting Systems Against Malware
category: Requirement 5: Protect All Systems and Networks from Malicious Software
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting all systems and networks from malicious software are defined and understood.
purpose: Ensures anti-malware policies are documented and maintained.
implementationGuidance: Define anti-malware policy covering all system components. Assign ownership. Review annually or when significant threat changes occur.
bestPractices: Align anti-malware policy with current threat intelligence
Review policy after major malware incidents affecting the industry
Include specific guidance for each system type

title: Malware Defences Deployed and Active
category: Requirement 5: Protect All Systems and Networks from Malicious Software
description: Malware is prevented, or detected and addressed.
purpose: Ensures technical controls actively protect all systems from malware.
implementationGuidance: Deploy anti-malware on all system components, particularly those at higher risk. Keep anti-malware updated. Enable real-time protection. Conduct periodic scans. Evaluate applicability to all system types.
bestPractices: Achieve 100% anti-malware coverage on all applicable systems
Monitor anti-malware health and update status continuously
Use behavioural detection capabilities in addition to signature-based detection

title: Anti-Malware Mechanisms and Processes Are Active, Maintained, and Monitored
category: Requirement 5: Protect All Systems and Networks from Malicious Software
description: Anti-malware mechanisms and processes are active, maintained, and monitored.
purpose: Ensures anti-malware effectiveness is sustained over time.
implementationGuidance: Configure anti-malware to run continuously and automatically. Prevent users from disabling it. Monitor anti-malware status across all endpoints. Alert on anti-malware failures or outdated signatures.
evidenceExamples: Anti-malware console showing all endpoints protected
bestPractices: Use centralised management console for all anti-malware
Alert immediately on any endpoint without current anti-malware protection
Investigate and remediate anti-malware failures within defined SLA

title: Anti-Phishing Mechanisms
category: Requirement 5: Protect All Systems and Networks from Malicious Software
description: Anti-phishing mechanisms protect users against phishing attacks.
purpose: Reduces the risk of credential theft and malware delivery via phishing.
implementationGuidance: Implement anti-phishing technical controls (email authentication — DMARC, SPF, DKIM; email security gateway). Provide phishing awareness training. Conduct phishing simulations.
evidenceExamples: Phishing simulation results and training completion records
bestPractices: Implement DMARC in enforce mode (p=reject)
Conduct quarterly phishing simulations
Provide immediate training to users who click on phishing simulations

title: Processes to Identify Security Vulnerabilities in Software
category: Requirement 6: Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software
description: Processes and mechanisms for protecting all systems and software from known vulnerabilities are defined and understood.
purpose: Ensures vulnerability management policies for software are defined and maintained.
implementationGuidance: Define vulnerability management policies covering software vulnerability identification, assessment, and remediation. Subscribe to security information sources. Define patch management SLAs. Review annually.
bestPractices: Subscribe to NVD, vendor security bulletins, and threat intelligence feeds
Define patch SLAs: critical within 24h, high within 7 days, medium within 30 days
Review patch SLAs quarterly against emerging threats

title: Bespoke and Custom Software Security
category: Requirement 6: Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software
description: Bespoke and custom software are developed securely.
purpose: Ensures internally developed software is built with security controls from the start.
implementationGuidance: Implement secure software development lifecycle (SSDLC). Apply OWASP secure coding practices. Conduct security testing. Implement code review. Train developers on secure coding.
evidenceExamples: Security testing results for in-house software
bestPractices: Train all developers on OWASP Top 10
Integrate SAST into CI/CD pipeline
Require code review for all changes to payment-related code

title: Security Vulnerabilities Are Identified and Addressed
category: Requirement 6: Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software
description: Security vulnerabilities are identified and protected against.
purpose: Ensures all software vulnerabilities are identified and remediated within defined timeframes.
implementationGuidance: Implement vulnerability scanning for all system components. Define remediation SLAs by severity. Track vulnerabilities to closure. Conduct penetration testing annually. Prioritise critical and exploitable vulnerabilities.
evidenceExamples: Critical vulnerability remediation evidence within SLA
bestPractices: Scan internet-facing systems continuously, internal systems at least weekly
Remediate critical vulnerabilities within 24 hours
Conduct annual penetration testing by qualified testers

title: Public-Facing Web Applications Are Protected Against Attacks
category: Requirement 6: Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software
description: Public-facing web applications are protected against attacks.
purpose: Ensures web applications handling cardholder data are protected from web-based attacks.
implementationGuidance: Deploy a Web Application Firewall (WAF) in front of all public-facing web applications processing CHD. Alternatively, conduct annual penetration testing of all public-facing web applications and remediate all vulnerabilities. The WAF approach provides continuous protection.
requiredDocuments: OR annual penetration test reports for web applications
evidenceExamples: WAF deployment evidence for CHD-processing web apps
OR penetration test reports with vulnerability remediation evidence
bestPractices: Deploy WAF in blocking mode (not detection-only) for CHD-processing applications
Tune WAF rules to minimise false positives while maintaining protection
Test WAF effectiveness as part of annual penetration testing

title: Changes to All System Components Are Managed Securely
category: Requirement 6: Develop and Maintain Secure Systems and Software
description: Changes to all system components are managed securely.
purpose: Ensures changes do not introduce security vulnerabilities or disrupt PCI controls.
implementationGuidance: Implement formal change control procedures for all in-scope systems. Require security impact assessment for changes. Test changes before deployment. Implement rollback procedures. Maintain change documentation.
bestPractices: Require separate developer and operations sign-off for production changes
Test all changes in a representative non-production environment first
Conduct post-implementation review for significant changes

title: Processes for Access Control to System Components and Cardholder Data
category: Requirement 7: Restrict Access to System Components and Cardholder Data by Business Need to Know
description: Processes and mechanisms for restricting access to system components and cardholder data by business need to know are defined and understood.
purpose: Establishes the policy basis for need-to-know access control to cardholder data.
implementationGuidance: Define access control policies based on the principle of least privilege and need to know. Document access control requirements. Review annually.
bestPractices: Apply least privilege as a default principle for all CHD access
Document the business justification for each role's access to CHD
Review access control policy after significant organisational changes

title: Access to System Components and Data Is Appropriately Defined and Assigned
category: Requirement 7: Restrict Access to System Components and Cardholder Data by Business Need to Know
description: Access to system components and data is appropriately defined and assigned.
purpose: Ensures access rights are based on job function and only provide what is needed.
implementationGuidance: Define access levels for each role based on specific job function. Implement RBAC. Do not grant administrative access without specific need. Require documented approval for all access assignments.
bestPractices: Define roles based on job function, not individuals
Require manager approval for all CHD access
Review role definitions annually for continued appropriateness

title: Access to System Components and Data Is Managed via an Access Control System(s)
category: Requirement 7: Restrict Access to System Components and Cardholder Data by Business Need to Know
description: Access to system components and data is managed via an access control system(s).
purpose: Ensures access is technically enforced and controlled systematically.
implementationGuidance: Implement an access control system that enforces defined access based on need-to-know. Default access should deny-all unless explicitly permitted. Automate access provisioning and deprovisioning where possible.
bestPractices: Implement IAM system with RBAC enforcement for all CHD systems
Configure default-deny access across all in-scope systems
Automate access provisioning tied to HR system for accuracy

title: Processes for Identifying Users and Authenticating Access
category: Requirement 8: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
description: Processes and mechanisms for identifying users and authenticating access to system components are defined and understood.
purpose: Establishes the policy basis for user identification and authentication controls.
implementationGuidance: Define authentication policies covering all user types (end users, admins, service accounts, third parties). Specify authentication requirements for each type. Review annually.
bestPractices: Define distinct authentication requirements for users, admins, and service accounts
Require MFA for all administrative access
Review authentication policy after significant authentication incidents

title: User Identification and Related Accounts for Users and Administrators Are Strictly Managed
category: Requirement 8: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
description: User identification and related accounts for users and administrators are strictly managed throughout an account's lifecycle.
purpose: Ensures each user is uniquely identified and accounts are properly managed.
implementationGuidance: Assign a unique ID to each user. Prohibit sharing of user IDs. Implement formal processes for account creation, modification, and deletion. Conduct periodic account reviews.
evidenceExamples: Account creation and deletion audit trails
bestPractices: Prohibit use of shared or generic accounts for human users
Audit active accounts quarterly against current personnel list
Implement automated deactivation upon termination

title: User Authentication for Users and Administrators Is Established and Managed
category: Requirement 8: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
description: User authentication for users and administrators is established and managed via an identity and authentication management system/process.
purpose: Ensures authentication credentials are configured and managed securely.
implementationGuidance: Implement strong password policies (minimum length, complexity, history). Lock accounts after 10 or fewer failed attempts. Require password change at first use and when compromised. Implement MFA for all remote access and all non-consumer access to the CDE.
evidenceExamples: MFA implementation evidence for all remote and admin access
bestPractices: Require minimum 12-character passwords with complexity
Implement MFA for all access to the CDE
Consider implementing passwordless authentication for higher security

title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Is Implemented to Secure Access into the CDE
category: Requirement 8: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
description: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is implemented to secure access into the cardholder data environment.
purpose: Provides a strong second layer of authentication for CDE access.
implementationGuidance: Implement MFA for all access into the CDE (interactive logins and non-console administrative access). Use at least two of the three factors: something you know, something you have, something you are. Do not allow user bypass of MFA.
evidenceExamples: MFA enforcement evidence for all CDE access
MFA configuration showing all three factor types available
bestPractices: Implement phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) for highest security
Apply MFA to all CDE access without exception
Alert on MFA failures or bypass attempts

title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Systems Are Configured to Prevent Misuse
category: Requirement 8: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
description: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) systems are configured to prevent misuse.
purpose: Ensures MFA implementation is resistant to common attack techniques.
implementationGuidance: Configure MFA to resist brute force, replay, and MFA fatigue attacks. Implement number matching and additional context for push notifications. Limit failed MFA attempts.
bestPractices: Enable number matching in push notifications to prevent MFA fatigue attacks
Limit push notification resends to prevent bombing attacks
Alert on multiple failed MFA attempts

title: Use of Application and System Accounts and Associated Authentication Factors Is Strictly Managed
category: Requirement 8: Identify Users and Authenticate Access to System Components
description: Use of application and system accounts and associated authentication factors is strictly managed.
purpose: Ensures service accounts and application credentials are securely managed.
implementationGuidance: Manage all system and application accounts with strong authentication. Store application credentials securely (not in code or config files). Rotate service account credentials regularly. Implement privileged access management for service accounts.
evidenceExamples: Service account inventory with managed credentials
No hardcoded credentials in code evidence
bestPractices: Use secrets management tools (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) for application credentials
Never hardcode credentials in source code
Review and rotate service account credentials at least annually

title: Processes for Restricting Physical Access
category: Requirement 9: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data
description: Processes and mechanisms for restricting physical access to cardholder data are defined and understood.
purpose: Ensures physical security policies for cardholder data environments are documented.
implementationGuidance: Define physical security policies covering the CDE and all cardholder data storage. Assign physical security responsibilities. Review annually.
bestPractices: Define distinct physical security requirements for CDE vs. general areas
Review physical security policy after any physical security incidents
Include physical security in all new facility planning

title: Physical Access Controls Manage Entry into Facilities and Systems Containing Cardholder Data
category: Requirement 9: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data
description: Physical access controls manage entry into facilities and systems containing cardholder data.
purpose: Prevents unauthorised individuals from accessing physical cardholder data environments.
implementationGuidance: Implement physical entry controls for CDE areas (electronic badges, biometrics, keypads). Verify visitor identity, log all visits, and escort visitors at all times. Conduct physical access reviews periodically.
bestPractices: Use multi-factor physical access (badge + PIN) for CDE areas
Review physical access logs weekly for CDE areas
Conduct quarterly reviews of who has physical access to the CDE

title: Physical Access for Personnel and Visitors Is Authorised and Managed
category: Requirement 9: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data
description: Physical access for personnel and visitors is authorised and managed.
purpose: Ensures only authorised individuals have physical access to cardholder data areas.
implementationGuidance: Authorise access before granting physical access. Issue badges distinguishing employees from visitors. Revoke access immediately upon termination. Conduct annual access reviews.
evidenceExamples: Badge types distinguishing employees and visitors
bestPractices: Automate badge revocation upon termination
Conduct quarterly reviews of physical access rights
Implement anti-tailgating measures for CDE entry points

title: Media With Cardholder Data Is Securely Stored, Accessed, Distributed, and Destroyed
category: Requirement 9: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data
description: Media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed.
purpose: Prevents loss or theft of cardholder data from physical media.
implementationGuidance: Store all media with cardholder data in physically secure, locked storage. Log and control access to media. Secure media during distribution. Destroy media securely when no longer needed.
evidenceExamples: Certificates of destruction for disposed media
bestPractices: Classify and label all media containing CHD
Conduct periodic inventory of all CHD media
Use certified destruction services for physical media disposal

title: Point-of-Interaction (POI) Devices Are Protected from Tampering and Unauthorised Substitution
category: Requirement 9: Restrict Physical Access to Cardholder Data
description: Point-of-interaction (POI) devices are protected from tampering and unauthorised substitution.
purpose: Prevents skimming attacks and physical tampering of payment card readers.
implementationGuidance: Maintain a device inventory of all POI devices. Inspect devices regularly for tampering. Train staff to recognise tampered devices. Verify device serial numbers against inventory before use.
evidenceExamples: POI device inventory with serial numbers
Staff training records on tampering detection
bestPractices: Inspect POI devices at start of each shift
Train all staff handling POI devices to check for tampering signs
Use tamper-evident seals on POI devices where possible

title: Processes for Logging and Monitoring All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Processes and mechanisms for logging and monitoring all access to system components and cardholder data are defined and understood.
purpose: Establishes the policy basis for logging and monitoring controls.
implementationGuidance: Define logging and monitoring policies covering all CDE system components. Specify what events to log. Define log retention requirements (at least 12 months, 3 months immediately available). Review annually.
bestPractices: Define logging requirements for all in-scope system types
Implement centralised logging via SIEM
Align log retention with PCI DSS requirement (12 months)

title: Audit Logs Capture All Individual User Access to Cardholder Data
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Audit logs capture all individual user access to cardholder data.
purpose: Creates accountability for all access to cardholder data through comprehensive audit trails.
implementationGuidance: Configure all CDE systems to log: user access to CHD, administrative actions, access to audit logs, failed logical access attempts, use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, system-level object access.
requiredDocuments: Audit log configuration for CDE systems
evidenceExamples: Audit log configuration showing all required events
bestPractices: Use SIEM to centralise and correlate all CDE audit logs
Define and test alert rules for suspicious access patterns
Review all CDE admin actions daily

title: Audit Logs Are Protected from Destruction and Unauthorised Modifications
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Audit logs are protected from destruction and unauthorised modifications.
purpose: Ensures audit logs remain intact and trustworthy for investigation and compliance evidence.
implementationGuidance: Protect audit logs from modification or deletion. Immediately copy logs to a separate, secure system. Restrict log access to security/audit personnel only. Implement integrity checking on critical logs.
evidenceExamples: SIEM or log management system access controls
bestPractices: Use immutable storage or WORM media for audit logs
Alert immediately on any attempt to modify or delete audit logs
Store logs in a system separate from the systems being logged

title: Audit Logs Are Reviewed to Identify Anomalies or Suspicious Activity
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Audit logs are reviewed to identify anomalies or suspicious activity.
purpose: Ensures security events are detected through systematic log review.
implementationGuidance: Implement daily review of CDE audit logs, particularly for privileged actions, authentication events, and suspicious activity. Use automated tools (SIEM) with correlation rules and alerts. Document review evidence.
bestPractices: Implement automated SIEM alerting to supplement manual log review
Review all privileged actions in the CDE daily
Escalate and investigate all suspicious log entries within defined SLAs

title: Audit Log History Is Retained for at Least 12 Months
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Retain audit log history for at least 12 months, with at least the most recent three months available for immediate analysis.
purpose: Ensures sufficient log history is available for investigations and forensic analysis.
implementationGuidance: Configure log retention for at least 12 months. Maintain at least 3 months of logs immediately accessible for real-time analysis. Archive older logs securely. Test log retrieval from archive.
bestPractices: Retain logs for longer than 12 months where possible (2–3 years) for forensic investigations
Test archive retrieval quarterly to ensure logs are accessible when needed
Implement automated archival with integrity verification

title: Time Synchronisation Technology
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Time-synchronisation technology supports consistent time settings across all systems.
purpose: Ensures log timestamps are accurate and consistent for audit and forensic purposes.
implementationGuidance: Implement NTP with a reliable, authoritative time source across all CDE systems. Define acceptable time drift thresholds. Monitor for NTP failures. Restrict who can change time settings on CDE systems.
evidenceExamples: NTP server configuration for all CDE systems
bestPractices: Use GPS-based stratum 1 NTP for highest accuracy
Monitor all CDE systems for NTP synchronisation failures
Alert on time changes outside the normal synchronisation cycle

title: Failures of Critical Security Controls Are Detected, Reported, and Responded to Promptly
category: Requirement 10: Log and Monitor All Access to System Components and Cardholder Data
description: Failures of critical security controls are detected, reported, and responded to promptly.
purpose: Ensures security control failures are identified and remediated quickly.
implementationGuidance: Implement monitoring for failures of all critical security controls (anti-malware, firewalls, IDS, audit logging, access controls, segmentation). Define response procedures for each failure type. Establish SLAs for response.
bestPractices: Monitor security control health continuously with automated alerting
Define maximum acceptable downtime for each critical control
Test control failure detection by simulating failures in non-production

title: Processes to Test Security of Systems and Networks
category: Requirement 11: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
description: Processes and mechanisms for regularly testing security of systems and networks are defined and understood.
purpose: Establishes the policy basis for security testing activities.
implementationGuidance: Define security testing policies covering all required testing types (vulnerability scans, penetration tests, IDS/IPS tests). Assign ownership. Define testing frequencies. Review annually.
bestPractices: Define testing requirements for each system type
Align testing frequency with risk level
Review testing results in management meetings

title: Wireless Access Points Are Identified and Monitored, and Unauthorised Wireless Access Points Are Addressed
category: Requirement 11: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
description: Wireless access points are identified and monitored, and unauthorised wireless access points are addressed.
purpose: Detects and responds to rogue wireless access points that could expose CHD.
implementationGuidance: Implement wireless intrusion detection. Conduct quarterly wireless scans to identify authorised and rogue access points. Investigate and remove all unauthorised wireless access points within a defined timeframe.
evidenceExamples: Rogue AP investigation and removal records
bestPractices: Deploy a dedicated WIDS for continuous rogue AP detection
Conduct wireless scans from multiple vantage points
Define maximum time to remove rogue APs (e.g., 24 hours)

title: External and Internal Vulnerabilities Are Regularly Identified, Prioritised, and Addressed
category: Requirement 11: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
description: External and internal vulnerabilities are regularly identified, prioritised, and addressed.
purpose: Ensures vulnerabilities are systematically identified through scanning and addressed based on risk.
implementationGuidance: Conduct internal vulnerability scans at least quarterly. Conduct external vulnerability scans by an ASV at least quarterly. Address all high and critical vulnerabilities. Conduct scans after significant changes.
evidenceExamples: ASV quarterly scan reports and attestation
bestPractices: Scan after every significant change, not just quarterly
Use the same qualified ASV for consistent scan results
Track mean time to remediate by vulnerability severity

title: External and Internal Penetration Testing Is Regularly Performed
category: Requirement 11: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
description: External and internal penetration testing is regularly performed, and exploitable vulnerabilities and security weaknesses are corrected.
purpose: Validates effectiveness of security controls through simulated adversary testing.
implementationGuidance: Conduct penetration tests at least annually and after any significant infrastructure or application changes. Use a qualified penetration tester (internal or external). Test both network and application layer. Remediate all exploitable vulnerabilities.
requiredDocuments: Penetration test reports and remediation records
evidenceExamples: Remediation tracking for pen test findings
bestPractices: Use qualified external penetration testers for annual tests
Test both external and internal perspectives
Retest all high-severity findings to verify remediation

title: Network Intrusions and Unexpected File Changes Are Detected and Responded To
category: Requirement 11: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
description: Network intrusions and unexpected file changes are detected and responded to.
purpose: Detects attacks and unauthorised changes to CDE systems in real time.
implementationGuidance: Deploy intrusion detection/prevention systems on the CDE network perimeter and critical segments. Implement a change detection mechanism (file integrity monitoring — FIM) on critical system files, configuration files, and application executables. Alert and investigate all detections.
requiredDocuments: Intrusion and change detection response procedures
bestPractices: Deploy IDS in both network and host modes (NIDS and HIDS)
Implement FIM on all critical CDE system files
Alert in real time on FIM detections and investigate within 1 hour

title: Unauthorised Changes on Payment Pages Are Detected and Responded To
category: Requirement 11: Test Security of Systems and Networks Regularly
description: Unauthorised changes on payment pages are detected and responded to.
purpose: Detects web skimming attacks targeting payment page content in real time.
implementationGuidance: Implement a mechanism to detect unauthorised modifications to payment pages (HTTP headers, scripts, inline content). This is a new requirement in PCI DSS v4.0 specifically addressing Magecart-style attacks. Evaluate and deploy CSP and SRI.
bestPractices: Implement strict Content Security Policy (CSP) headers on payment pages
Use Subresource Integrity (SRI) for all third-party scripts
Deploy dedicated e-commerce security monitoring tools

title: Comprehensive Security Policy
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: A comprehensive information security policy is defined, published, maintained, and disseminated.
purpose: Establishes the overarching security policy framework for PCI DSS compliance.
implementationGuidance: Develop and publish a comprehensive information security policy. Ensure it is reviewed at least annually and updated after significant security changes or incidents. Disseminate to all relevant personnel.
bestPractices: Review the security policy at least annually and after significant incidents
Obtain management sign-off on the policy at each review
Include all PCI DSS requirements in scope of the security policy

title: Acceptable Use Policies
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are documented and implemented.
purpose: Defines acceptable use of technology resources to reduce risk.
implementationGuidance: Develop AUPs covering all end-user technologies including computers, mobile devices, email, and internet. Require personnel to read and accept AUPs annually. Enforce through technical controls where possible.
bestPractices: Include specific guidance on handling CHD in the AUP
Update AUP when technology environments change significantly
Enforce AUP provisions technically where possible

title: Risks to the Cardholder Data Environment Are Formally Identified, Evaluated, and Managed
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Risks to the cardholder data environment are formally identified, evaluated, and managed.
purpose: Ensures a structured risk management approach is applied to CDE risks.
implementationGuidance: Conduct an annual formal risk assessment of the CDE. Use a consistent risk assessment methodology. Document identified risks with likelihood, impact, and treatment plans. Review risks when the environment changes significantly.
evidenceExamples: Annual risk assessment for the CDE
bestPractices: Conduct risk assessments at least annually and after significant changes
Involve business stakeholders in the risk assessment process
Track risk treatment plan completion and re-assess residual risk

title: PCI DSS Compliance Is Managed
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: PCI DSS compliance is managed throughout the year.
purpose: Ensures PCI DSS is treated as a continuous programme rather than an annual point-in-time exercise.
implementationGuidance: Implement a continuous compliance monitoring programme. Assign compliance ownership to executive management. Conduct quarterly reviews of compliance status. Implement processes to respond to non-compliance promptly.
bestPractices: Treat PCI DSS compliance as continuous, not annual
Implement automated compliance monitoring across CDE controls
Report compliance status to executive management quarterly

title: PCI DSS Scope Is Documented and Validated
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: PCI DSS scope is documented and validated at least annually and upon significant changes to the in-scope environment.
purpose: Ensures the PCI DSS assessment scope accurately reflects the cardholder data environment.
implementationGuidance: Conduct an annual scoping exercise to identify all in-scope systems. Document the scope with network diagrams and data flows. Validate the scope with the QSA. Review scope after any significant environmental changes.
evidenceExamples: Scope documentation with CHD data flows
bestPractices: Document scope with precise network diagrams and data flow diagrams
Review scope whenever new systems or processes are added
Validate scope with QSA as part of annual assessment preparation

title: Security Awareness Education
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Security awareness education is an ongoing activity.
purpose: Ensures all personnel understand their role in protecting cardholder data.
implementationGuidance: Implement a security awareness programme with at least annual training for all personnel. Include PCI DSS-specific content. Conduct phishing simulations. Provide specialised training for roles with specific PCI DSS responsibilities.
bestPractices: Make PCI DSS training role-specific and relevant
Conduct phishing simulations quarterly
Track comprehension with post-training assessments

title: Personnel Are Screened to Reduce Risks from Insider Threats
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Personnel are screened to reduce risks from insider threats.
purpose: Reduces the risk of hiring individuals who could misuse access to cardholder data.
implementationGuidance: Conduct background checks for all personnel with access to the CDE. Screen to the extent permitted by local law and commensurate with the sensitivity of the role. Document screening procedures.
requiredDocuments: Background check policy for CDE personnel
evidenceExamples: Policy defining screening requirements for CDE roles
bestPractices: Screen all personnel with any access to CHD, not just those in dedicated security roles
Re-screen personnel moving into higher-sensitivity roles
Use accredited third-party screening services

title: Risk to Information Assets Is Managed
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Risk associated with third-party service providers (TPSPs) with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of account data is managed.
purpose: Ensures third-party risks to cardholder data are identified and managed.
implementationGuidance: Implement a TPSP management programme. Maintain a list of all TPSPs. Perform due diligence before engagement and annually. Ensure TPSPs acknowledge responsibility for security of shared data. Monitor TPSP compliance.
evidenceExamples: TPSP written acknowledgements of PCI DSS responsibilities
bestPractices: Request TPSP AOC (Attestation of Compliance) annually
Include PCI DSS responsibilities in TPSP contracts
Monitor TPSP security incidents through news and intelligence sources

title: Third-Party Service Providers (TPSPs) Support Their Customers' PCI DSS Compliance
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Third-party service providers (TPSPs) support their customers' PCI DSS compliance efforts.
purpose: Clarifies TPSP obligations to support customer PCI DSS compliance.
implementationGuidance: Acknowledge to customers the PCI DSS requirements the TPSP owns. Provide customers with information about the TPSP's PCI DSS responsibilities. Maintain clear contracts defining which party is responsible for each PCI DSS requirement.
evidenceExamples: Contracts with PCI DSS responsibility delineation
Customer AOC or letter of compliance
bestPractices: Publish a clear responsibility matrix for all shared requirements
Provide customers with your current AOC annually
Review and update responsibility matrices when services change

title: Suspected and Confirmed Security Incidents That Could Impact the CDE Are Responded To Immediately
category: Requirement 12: Support Information Security with Organisational Policies and Programs
description: Suspected and confirmed security incidents that could impact the CDE are responded to immediately.
purpose: Ensures security incidents are handled effectively to protect cardholder data.
implementationGuidance: Define and implement an incident response plan covering suspected and confirmed incidents affecting the CDE. Test the plan at least annually. Cover payment card brand incident notification requirements. Address forensic investigation procedures.
bestPractices: Test the incident response plan with both tabletop and live exercises annually
Include card brand notification requirements in the IRP
Define maximum time to notify card brands of confirmed breaches (typically 24 hours)
